Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I work part-time to cover my expenses, so I'd squeeze in study time whenever I could - sometimes early mornings before a shift, sometimes late at night. I tried to dedicate about 34 hours a week to studying, but it was rarely in consistent blocks. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often pace around my room reading notes aloud or work through problems physically rather than just staring at a screen.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life. When deadlines hit, I'd go into overdrive and study for hours straight. But during quieter weeks, I might spread things out more. Work schedules changed constantly, so I couldn't really commit to studying at the same time every week. I adapted as best I could, but regular times just weren't realistic for me.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something due soon or when I got a notification about new content. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if there were any announcements I'd missed since I couldn't always make it to class. If I heard classmates talking about something posted online, that would prompt me to go look. It wasn't really a daily habit, more reactive than proactive.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where I got into a groove of checking the LMS every couple of days. I'd made a study group with some peers, and we'd coordinate through the discussion boards, so that gave me a reason to log in regularly. But then my work hours increased and that routine fell apart. It never quite became automatic for me like it seemed to be for some other students.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I could pause and rewind when something didn't click. I watched them for over an hour usually because I'd replay sections multiple times. The discussion boards were also useful because interacting with peers helped me understand different perspectives on the material. When you're from a low-resource background like me, having free access to quality video content is actually huge.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited time, I had to prioritize what seemed most essential for passing. Some resources felt redundant or too text-heavy when I learn better by doing things hands-on. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came with everything else going on.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I could apply something practically or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, I was all in. I also valued activities where I could interact with others - the peer interactions kept me motivated. Anything that felt like busy work without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different - they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required stuff had to get done no matter what, so I'd prioritize those even when exhausted. With optional activities, I'd weigh whether the time investment would actually help me or if I should use that time to work an extra shift. Sometimes I felt guilty skipping them, but survival mode meant making tough choices.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my phone so I could study during my bus commute to work. I borrowed textbooks from the library and worked through problems by hand in a notebook. I also used free YouTube tutorials and Khan Academy to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials alone.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely engaged but it wouldn't show online. I'd print out materials at the library and study at work during slow periods. My study group would meet in person at the campus café, working through problems on whiteboards. I'd also quiz myself using flashcards I made by hand - old school, but it works for how I learn. None of that leaves a digital trace.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then review those notes repeatedly offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so working offline was often more practical anyway. I'd return to the same handwritten notes for weeks without ever logging back into the LMS to view the original content again.
Q12: Things got really hard around midterms when my work increased my hours right when assignments piled up. I was exhausted and had to miss more classes than I wanted - my attendance dropped significantly. There was a point where I felt overwhelmed and surprised by how quickly I'd fallen behind. The mindfulness exercises they suggested actually helped me manage the stress somewhat, but catching up felt impossible.
Q13: Definitely. When I was mentally drained from a long shift, I'd log into the LMS but just couldn't absorb anything. Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor - choosing between studying and earning money to pay rent isn't really a choice. Confusion about some course concepts made me avoid certain modules because I didn't know where to start. Fatigue made even simple tasks feel monumental.
Q14: There were times I'd have videos playing while doing other things, not really absorbing the content. I'd scroll through readings without actually processing the words because my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or work. Sometimes I'd open the LMS just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't truly engaged. It was more about easing guilt than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was intimidation - they felt overwhelming when I was already stretched thin. I'd convince myself I'd do better work if I waited until I had more time or energy, but that time never materialized. The anxiety of starting something I might fail at made avoidance feel safer in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd have bursts of intense engagement right before something was due. This meant my login frequency spiked around deadline periods and dropped during quieter times. It wasn't ideal for deep learning, but it was how I survived. I wished I could be more consistent, but deadlines were the external pressure I needed to push through.
Q17: When I got feedback showing I'd done well on an assignment, it motivated me to engage more with similar content. My assignment scores were actually decent when I submitted work, which surprised me and gave me hope. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and drift. Positive feedback reminded me I was capable despite everything working against me.
Q18: The course structure with so many online components actually helped me since I couldn't always attend in person. Having 15 online course modules meant flexibility, which I needed. But the structure also assumed consistent engagement that I couldn't maintain. When modules built on each other and I'd missed earlier content, catching up became exponentially harder.
Q19: The flexibility of online materials made engagement easier because I could study whenever possible. But the sheer volume of content was overwhelming - it was hard to know what to prioritize. Discussion boards were great for connection, but sometimes responses came too slow to help when I was stuck. Clearer guidance on what was essential versus supplementary would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading, watching, and participating but hitting walls when it comes to actually completing assignments. Maybe they understand concepts but can't translate that into written submissions. Or life circumstances might allow for browsing but not for the focused time needed to complete substantial work.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My assignment scores were pretty good when I actually submitted work, even though my overall LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline and through peer study groups that wouldn't show up in logs. Someone could be efficiently using downloaded materials and external resources while barely touching the LMS. Activity doesn't equal learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and for how long, plus participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't capture offline studying, the thinking that happens away from the screen, or the depth of understanding someone develops. They also miss context - why someone's activity dropped or what barriers they're facing.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and wanting to understand it, not just go through the motions. It's about connecting concepts to real applications and caring about mastering the content. For me, engagement also involves learning with others - those 33 peer interactions meant a lot to me. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time logged on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite financial stress and work demands. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent on buses with downloaded materials or the study sessions at my kitchen table with borrowed textbooks. It can't measure my resilience or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage stress. My engagement was real, even when my data looked sparse.
